speaker: interviewer
text: Mila Bachner, July 25, 1995-- Mila, you were telling me that your mother gave you some advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we did-- we did dig ditches, and we hid in them. Bombs started to fly over Poland, of course. This is something everybody knows. And we never knew where the bombs will hit and so we stayed in the ditches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, my-- I remember my family, my relatives got together with other townspeople and they decided that we are going to evacuate somewhere to safety. And where would the safety be? They were talking about Russia. I wish we'd have made it to Russia. Perhaps we would have survived. But-- and it didn't take long. We-- we got cart-- the horse-- the horses we had but the carts we got like wagons. And we put on those-- on those wagons things. You know, whenever I talk about this, I remember Anatevka in because this is what we looked like. Did you see Remember Anatevka, when the Jews started to evacuate from Russia? But they went to America. We did not know where we're going. We were going to the slaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started our journey. We took the children and whatever clothes necessary, whatever we could scrape together on food, and we started out. It was a long chain of evacuees. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long? How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about-- how many people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my-- on our cart, we had our eight people. It was my sister already with her husband because she had gotten married. I-- I failed to tell you-- I should digress for just a moment because my sister Malka was a magnificent looking girl. She was very beautiful, and she won a beauty contest in [INAUDIBLE] in 1936 from [INAUDIBLE]. This was the highest school of-- of-- it would be like high school in Hebrew, I would say. And she was only 18 years old. And when we-- and my mommy said to her, Malka, you are going to get married to this boy, to Rolf Goldstein. But she says, Mommy, I don't really love him. She said, Malka, love should not be now the-- the question because if you do have a husband, when the Germans come in, they will not touch a married woman. And under this pretext-- I don't know if it was a pretext or Mommy really believed in what she said. She probably believed, and my sister got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we already had her husband, and I think that she was already pregnant at that time because the baby came soon thereabout, I think, early December-- September, October, November, December. The baby came some times later on. And-- and in 1940, the baby came, I think. And the evacuation, less than about six weeks, we were-- So you were all on this train and how many wagons were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were so many families because at that time, everybody still lived in town. Nobody was-- the Germans had not invaded our town, at least, yet. You know? But the invasion had already taken place. As I will go on, you'll see. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started-- we went through [? Plaszow, ?] through-- through different towns, Krakow-- we passed the Vistula, that big river Vistula that falls into the-- to the Baltic Ocean. There was low tide at one point and we crossed it, believe it. We were on the-- on the buggies and the older people, like my sister, my-- my dad, my mom, they would hold onto the-- to the-- to the carriage and the horses, which schlepped up. But it was at one point low tide, where you could swim along with the-- with the-- with the wagon and the horses. And we crossed this river. And then once we got there, it was already-- I-- when I said, about six weeks, we were met by the German tanks over there past the-- past the water. We took a different way to go home, you know, and we were forced by the Germans to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know what way we took. Don't ask me because I was too young even to know what was-- you know, how-- how, what, when. And as we are going back, you know, under the German guards with, you know, with their bayonets ready to-- you know, I could see dead bodies on the floor, laying on the ground, and I couldn't imagine why-- why there's dead bodies. And I said, Momma, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: I see dead people. And then, I said, Mommy, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This person is moving. We have to go help. So mommy said, Mila, look, don't-- [GERMAN]-- don't look, she said to me. Just be quiet and don't talk. Because she was afraid that they-- they took people off the wagons. They took people out of the houses, and they just shot them, just like what you saw in When I looked at this-- at these pictures, this is what I saw myself. This is what I lived. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people, the dead people, you knew them? They were from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I knew what they were doing. I knew that the Germans were shooting them. These people were from other towns and even from-- people that I knew, I wouldn't know if I knew them because I really didn't know everybody. I knew people from my street, mostly, you know. So one lady died before the war, which was great. And-- And then, I see little objects being thrown in the air, and-- and they were shooting at them and they were blown to pieces. And I-- and then, I'm observing closer and I see that these little-- that those were little babies. They were taking babies from-- from young mothers and throwing them in the air and they were using them for target practice. They were throwing them against the brick walls from the homes. Their little skulls split in two. Our little infants, our sweetness of life and joy, they were just like-- like-- I-- I don't think I could step on a bug as freely as what they did to our-- to our children. [SOBBING] I don't know if I could step on a bug as freely, I swear to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here, my sister was pregnant, and we have to worry that she should not be noticed by the Nazis. You know. Anyway, we did come home to our hometown, [? burg ?] Chrzanow, and the-- the town had been turned into a ghetto. I already saw people from different parts of the country, like from Katowice and Ciezkowice and the Bedzin, you know, surrounding towns. They already were brought to our town, Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did this with a reason. They wanted to have the Jews in one place, you know, and this way, they were easy-- it was easier for transportation. Rather than to go to each town, they had them together. And they were-- we were together in this-- my-- my apartment, as little as it was for us eight people, I think that there were about 25 people in this one apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our apartment, 25 people. There was no food. There was no water. The-- the toilet was [INAUDIBLE]. Every day, we used to go through outside to the backyard. There was a communal toilet, sort of. And babies cried because the mothers were-- didn't have enough food for them, little babies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I-- this one mother was sitting outside feeding her baby on the step, and this-- this SS man goes by. And he says, what are you doing? And she says, I am [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to her, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I'm giving my child to eat. He went over to close to her-- close to her, takes out a little bayonet, one of those short ones, and he cuts off the breast. And the baby is left with the breast in the mouth and the mother died. These are the-- these are the horrific stories that the Nazis did to our people, to our mothers, to our babies. Then, they had the trained dogs. This is a-- a later story, as I'm going on. And I came running home to Mommy and I told her. And she says, Mila, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Don't go out and don't look, please. Please just stay-- be still and don't let anybody see you. I tried very much to hold-- to-- to compose myself and not to do this, but I was young and I did not care. I was very-- I was inquisitive and sometimes to my disadvantage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went back-- I went back into the house and, you know, an epidemic broke out and the children started, from hunger and from-- from illness, from dirt, no diapers, no nothing, people got sick. So they had a good reason to exterminate people because they're spreading an epidemic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this made us-- this made us 'filthy Jews.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what they were sick with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dysentery-type--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dysenteries-- dysentery and-- [GERMAN]. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a certain-- where you ran out with blood. I don't know what you call this in English, but this-- we called it [GERMAN], the red illness. Everything-- everything was infected in your system and you-- they ran out of blood. And of course, this became-- and this is very highly contagious. There was no hospital. There was no doctor for us to come and give us a hand, help. We were just-- we were just destined. We were just destined to die. And then, an order came out over the loudspeaker that all Jews must wear the Stars of David, the yellow armband with the-- with the black Star of David that was inside or blue Star of David. We couldn't imagine why this would be, but again, this was to-- for easy selection from the rest of the herd, so to speak, for easy transportation to the death camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were other people living in town besides the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We had all these people that were caught evacuating-- were forced back to town. I don't know if there were any shot. I-- I didn't dare look around. When I looked at the ground, I saw enough. I already knew that the slaughter had begun. Nobody had to tell me this, you know. And I-- and-- And then, it really started. It was 1940. My-- my sister's baby was born, and she did not have a doctor. She gave birth in the house. I remember how she suffered because she needed a section and she couldn't have it. I don't know what this Dr. Bobshinski did, a Polish doctor that come because he liked my father a lot and  he came in secretly to her apartment and to help to deliver the baby without a section. But she was on the bottom, you know, and she suffered a great deal. But the baby was a beautiful healthy, little boy and they named him Avrom Herschel. My little grandson is named after him, [INAUDIBLE], my son's son. And-- and my sister had to work for the Germans. She used to shovel the snow from the mountains. She would come into our-- our apartment, however, and she would feed the baby. She would breastfeed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember once, one of the Germans came into the house, and he sees me cry. And he said, why are you crying? I-- are-- are you afraid? Are we barbarians or something? I said, no, I'm not crying. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I caught a cold and my eyes are tearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister said, Mila, [SPEAKING POLISH]. Go into-- go into the room. She like murmur-- you know, murmured it to me. He should not and he didn't understand. And I turned and I went into the little room where she and I slept before she was married, you know. And then came 19-- in 1940, my oldest brother Chaim-- I have him there-- he passes by our window. And he says to Mommy, Mama, I heard there is going to be a roundup on young boys. And I'm going to sleep to my friend's house on the attic. They have a big attic. Perhaps they will not find me. We kissed him goodbye through the window. He didn't come into the house. We just kissed him, and we wished him good luck and we said a prayer for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next morning, he passes by our window under the Gestapo guard with their crazy dogs at their side. The German shepherds were ready to attack and rip you apart command. I'm telling you, I've seen it happen. And he-- he passes by and he says to me, [? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy, they did get-- get us. And all those young boys from town, you know, were led to-- probably to Starachowice to the [? Dulag, ?] you know, which was a-- a camp for every prisoner every-- had to pass through this camp. My other brother did. I did. I found my other brother's signature, you know, handwriting-- his name on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We cried unbelievably. We just couldn't stop because you-- you have a family with beautiful children, parents. All of a sudden, they invade your homes. They invade your life. And they take your siblings away. I just wonder if people can-- if people can conceive, if they can humanly understand the pain to watch a thing like this happen. You know, I-- I-- I don't know. Were we really so inhuman? Who was the inhuman one, we or-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about two months later, three months later, we got word from that camp, [INAUDIBLE], that my brother died. He was killed. He was caught stealing a piece of bread, and they killed him for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the-- one of our friends came back from that camp. Whether he ran away, he came back to Chrzanow to his wife and to his two daughters. I'll show you the daughter on the wedding picture. And he told us that he was there and he saw it happen. He stole a piece of bread, and he-- and they killed him. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- Malka was-- if he was-- he was younger than my sister. I would say 16 years. I would say 16, maybe 16 and a half. And this was my brother number one. So you can imagine my mommy, how she broke down, how she never cried and we all cried. Then, I had my brother, [? Murray, ?] who survived, my only brother. He was taken from us in 1941. Again, he worked for the Germans in Chrzanow-- not in Chrzanow, in-- in-- in another town but I don't know what he did. They were looking for him. So he was hiding, but they caught him. And he was taken to Markstadt in Germany. This was brother number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came-- then, I started seeing roundups of the old and the sick. They threw those old rabbis on-- on-- on carts like you throw hay. You know, the-- the-- you know, how you call that when you pick up the corn, you know, and you throw it for-- after you harvest it? This is how they were throwing these people. The old and the sick were the first victims to go. This really happened in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I remember how they picked up 100 of the most prominent people in my hometown and they led them through [? Chebinya ?] behind-- to-- to a empty space, empty like a field. And then, at a-- a truck with shovels was going along. When they came to the destination, all these people-- and when I say, 'the most prominent,' I mean lawyers and doctors and professors and mathematicians and scientists, you name it-- 150 of them, and they-- they had to get off the trucks and under the-- under the Gestapo guard and their dogs, of course, that they assign. If they said-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], that dog ripped you apart to shreds. There was nothing left of you. And so this happened to a pregnant woman, and I'll tell you when and why. They had to-- get-- they were ordered to get undressed and fold their clothes neatly and empty out their pockets, whatever there is of value, to empty it out and put it in a box which was provided by the Gestapo. And-- and they put all these things in, including gold teeth, whatever was removed. You know. And then they were given a-- they were all stark naked, they were taken to shovels, and they are to dig a mass grave. When this was done, they were forced to face the ditch, and they were shot in the back into the ditches. One man managed to climb out. You know, they fell one on top of another. Even if the ditch was six feet tall, whatever, he could still manage to climb up and come out of the-- and come out of the grave. My cousin's father was among them but he was not shot. There were women also standing naked, but somehow, they did not kill the women at that point. There were stand-- there were women standing. He hid among the women, and this is how he went away-- went away with them, my uncle Joseph, the one who is with my father in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and how did you find out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my cousin, Charlie, whose father it was, he came home and he told the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, at that time, he came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time. Yeah, he made it home, my uncle. But one person who was-- who was mortally wounded managed to climb out of that. My-- my uncle was not in the-- in the ditch. He-- he-- he escaped from between the men while he was dressed. I don't know how it happened but he managed to hide behind the women. He-- Charlie told us that there were women. Uncle told him there were women standing. They were not killed at that point yet. They didn't do it like so quickly. This was-- you-- you-- maybe they were their wives and they had to watch because that's how they worked, whatever happened. You know. And this man made it out of the grave, and he made it home to his apartment on all fours. He died in the apartment. But he warned his family of what the Nazis are doing and what happened to the other people. This was the number-- this was the one thing that I-- this was number one. And then, there was a hanging of seven innocent people in my hometown, in Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This we had to watch. My-- my mommy was still home. My dad, [? Hamech, ?] and my brother [? Moishe ?] were not there anymore. My sister was still there, and she already had a baby at that point, you know. And my brother I didn't see at all because he was in hiding someplace. He-- they say that he stole something and so they looked for him, and they did not-- my brother-in-law but they did not need any excuses for killing a Jew, believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened is we had a baker in our town. His name was Mr. [? Gelsnaw, ?] [? Schmeal ?] [? Gelsnaw. ?] He owned a bakery, and we used to buy the breads there. He had one son who was not so OK mentally. He was-- he was a little bit retarded, you know, functioning but retarded. And he went into the bakery, and he turned on an oven. And the Gestapo saw the smoke come out of the chimney. So they went into the bakery, and he saw that boy there, that young boy, you know, and they accused him of baking bread so that he could supply bread for the Jewish people of the town. And then, all of a sudden, we hear over the loudspeaker that the, [SPEAKING GERMAN], that the whole town is to get together, that they are going to-- we are going down to the [GERMAN]-- my friend's-- my friend's backyard, the [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is where we are going to march down to this place. Of course, you obey. We went like little sheep to the slaughter, you know. And before we reach the street, maybe a mile away, you could hear the music play. And you know the music that they played was Mozart. Mostly, it was the Mozart music that they played and laughter and singing and-- and-- and music was blasting. When we came close, there was a whole bash going on, a whole party the Nazis had, table full of food. They were drinking, and they were going [NON-ENGLISH], nuts. And then, we see the gallows and seven nooses from the gallows, and of course, little boxes under each person to step on, you know, step on. And we are standing and each few people, there was a Gestapo with a dog looking to us to be sure that we do not avert our eyes, that we watch what is going to happen. There was one boy from Olkusz, not far from where I live, and I remember he was yelling from the-- from the gallows. He said, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Remember, Jews, I am going-- [SPEAKING HEBREW] is a sacrifice to God. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I am 18 years old, he said, and take revenge. And as this goes on, all of a sudden, one woman who was pregnant and she could not look at this senseless torture, she turned her head. One word that Gestapo said to that dog-- he went over to her, he ripped her stomach open and ripped out the baby, and they were both dead on the spot. This happened right there in the street, and I was there. OK. I-- I would have fallen down to faint, but I knew if I faint, I'm not going to live anymore. So I did not faint.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930-- this was in 1940 so I was 13, 13 and a half years old-- 13 and a half years old. I don't know what month, maybe 13 and two months or something. I was so devastated. I was in such agonizing pain. I could not believe what I saw. I couldn't-- I-- I-- I could not-- I could not believe what people can do to people. I-- I-- I couldn't believe it. My dad was then-- and then, of course, they kicked away the boxes and the heads fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was the father-- I forgot to tell you. There was a father with his two sons, the father who owned the bakery. One son was from Monowitz-Auschwitz, who came to Chrzanow-- he lived there before the war-- and this boy, [? Shamusan. ?] So the three of them died on the gallows. And then, there was this boy, 18 years old, so it's four and three other people, just people at random they picked for the-- for this-- for this hanging.

speaker: interviewee
text: My then-- then-- then, we had a Schindler also, by the way, but not the Schindler. His name was Schindler also but one of them from those-- from those Gestapo called Zagorski. They couldn't say Zagorski but 'Sagorski.' The 'z' was like I said there. And he ordered my dad to cut down the bodies. So I remember how gently my daddy had this knife in his hand. And he cut the rope and then he took the body and put it down. Then, he did the same to the second and gently put it down and then all seven were laid out. And this is all that we could do. There was no burial. There was nothing. If they picked up the bodies later and sent them to the factories where they make soaps, which they did, it's quite possible. There were other reasons they-- they used them, you know. Not reasons-- they used them for many important things, hair for the mattresses, lampshades and-- and soaps. Sometimes, they shipped it to Germany, the finest soaps they had, you know. I came home and I fell done on the floor and I-- and I was hardly-- you know, my mother hardly revived me already. She thought I'm dead. I could not-- what I saw, what I witnessed was already too much for me to bear. I-- I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a 19-- oh, I worked in [INAUDIBLE]. The pictures that I showed you have the little photo ID? This was the purpose of those IDs. We belonged to the [SPEAKING GERMAN], German government sort of, to speak. I was under their jurisdiction. I had this ID and with this ID, with many other girls, young girls, we worked in [INAUDIBLE] and we made raincoats for the German army. You know, they shouldn't catch a cold, god forbid, you know, the front up there. So I remember going to-- oh, my sister was gone already at that time. My sister Malka was gone. Let me tell you what happened with her. Then-- then there was a-- before this happened with me-- anyway, I worked in this factory. This-- I had a job, no pay, just work, day shifts and night shifts.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then came a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we hear again that that's going to be a roundup on young mothers with their babies. And my sister Malka already had this baby. He was eight months old, my little Avrom [INAUDIBLE], such a beautiful little thing. And-- and-- and there were beautiful mothers. There were the young mothers at home with their babies, little-- little-- little children a year old, [INAUDIBLE] on the arms, newborn. My sister-- I slept-- that night, I slept in my sister's house. And I had no place to go, anyway, you know, so I slept in her apartment. We had to round up outside. We could not take anything from the apartment. Everything was to-- to leave. It was confiscated by the Germans. The only thing that I took is my sister's coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he's--
